title: The IOB Tagging Format
concept: Inside-Outside-Beginning (IOB) tagging labels tokens to distinguish between single-word entities and multi-word entities (like 'New' and 'York').

exercises.prompt: Why do we need a special tagging format like IOB for words in a sentence labeling formats like IOB in natural language processing?

options.text: To show where a named entity starts and ends in a sentence
To translate English text into other languages
To correct spelling mistakes in the text

exercises.explain: Without a special format, the computer cannot tell if consecutive words are part of one long name or multiple separate names.

cards.title: Where Does the Name End?
cards.body: If you tell an AI to find cities in the sentence 'I want to visit New York and New Haven', how does it know that 'New York' is one city, but 'New' and 'Haven' belong together too, rather than being one giant city called 'New York and New Haven'? We need a tagging system to show the boundaries!

cards.title: What is IOB Tagging?
cards.body:
IOB stands for Inside, Outside, Beginning. It is a standard way to label words in a text so a computer knows where a named entity (like a person's name or a city) starts and ends. 

• B (Beginning) starts an entity.
• I (Inside) continues an entity.
• O (Outside) is for normal words.

cards.title: The Gift-Wrapping System
cards.body: Imagine you are wrapping a long gift that spans three separate boxes. On the first box, you stick a label that says 'START of Gift'. On the next two boxes, you stick labels that say 'CONTINUATION of Gift'. Any other random boxes that aren't part of the gift get a label saying 'NOT part of the gift'. This is exactly how IOB tagging labels words!

cards.title: IOB in Action
cards.body:
Let's tag the phrase 'Visit New York City' using IOB for locations:

• 'Visit' is not a location, so it gets: O
• 'New' starts the location name, so it gets: B-LOC
• 'York' continues the location name, so it gets: I-LOC
• 'City' continues the location name, so it gets: I-LOC

This tells the AI that 'New York City' is one single multi-word entity!

cards.title: Why Not Just Label Everything 'Entity'?
cards.body: You might think every word in a name just gets labeled as 'Entity'. But if we did that, the computer wouldn't know if 'New York' and 'New Haven' are two different cities next to each other, or just one big name. The 'B-' tag is crucial because it marks exactly where a brand new entity starts!

cards.title: B-LOC vs. I-LOC
cards.body: See how the tags change depending on whether a word starts a new entity or just continues one.

right.text: Used for any words that follow the first word inside the location, like 'York'.

left.text: Used for the very first word of a location name, like 'New' in 'New York'.

exercises.prompt: What does the letter 'O' stand for in the IOB tagging format?
exercises.explain: The 'O' tag stands for Outside, which means the word is not part of any named entity we care about.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A multi-word named entity can start directly with an 'I-' tag without using a 'B-' tag first.
exercises.explain: An 'I-' tag can only follow a 'B-' tag of the same type, or another 'I-' tag. You cannot start a brand new entity with 'I-'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct tag for the word 'Alice' in the name 'Alice Smith' (using PER for person).
exercises.sentence: The word 'Alice' gets labeled as [blank] to show it starts the name.
exercises.explain: Since 'Alice' is the first word of the person's name, it must start with the Beginning tag: B-PER. 'Smith' would then be I-PER.

exercises.prompt: Which AI output correctly tags 'Apple Inc.' as an organization?
exercises.explain: AI Option 1 is incorrect because it starts an entity with 'I-' instead of 'B-'. AI Option 3 labels 'Apple' as 'O', which misses the entity entirely. AI Option 2 correctly starts the entity with 'B-ORG' and continues it with 'I-ORG'.

exercises.prompt: Let's test your skills! What are the correct IOB tags for the first three words in the sentence: 'San Francisco is...' if we are looking for location entities?
exercises.explain: The word 'San' is the beginning of the location entity (B-LOC), 'Francisco' is the continuation of it (I-LOC), and 'is' is not part of any named entity (O).
